executive_summary: Implement secure user authentication with OAuth2.0, JWT tokens, and role-based access control (RBAC) to replace the legacy session-based authentication system.
goals: Replace legacy session-based auth with JWT tokens
success_criteria: All API endpoints protected with JWT authentication
OAuth2.0 integration with Google and GitHub
RBAC policies enforced across all services
risks: Token expiration handling may cause user session interruptions
OAuth2.0 provider downtime could block logins
Migration of existing users requires careful data handling
Performance degradation if Redis cache fails

steps.title: Design Authentication Architecture
steps.objective: Define complete authentication flow, data models, and API contracts
actions: Document JWT token structure (claims, expiration, refresh strategy)
Design OAuth2.0 flow diagrams for Google and GitHub
Define RBAC model (roles, permissions, resource mapping)
Create API contract for /auth/login, /auth/refresh, /auth/logout endpoints
Design database schema for users, roles, and permissions tables
validation: Architecture reviewed and approved by security team
API contracts documented in OpenAPI format
rollback: N/A - design phase only, no deployment

steps.title: Set Up Infrastructure
steps.objective: Provision and configure Redis cache, database tables, and OAuth2.0 credentials
actions: Provision Redis cluster (3 nodes, replication enabled)
Apply database migrations for user_roles, permissions tables
Register OAuth2.0 applications with Google and GitHub
Store OAuth2.0 client secrets in vault
Configure Redis eviction policy (LRU) and max memory
validation: OAuth2.0 test credentials work in staging
rollback: Destroy Redis cluster with terraform destroy

steps.title: Implement JWT Token Service
steps.objective: Build service for generating, validating, and refreshing JWT tokens
prerequisites: Token signing key generated and stored securely
actions: Implement token generation with user claims (id, email, roles)
Add token validation middleware for all protected endpoints
Implement refresh token flow with rotation
Add Redis caching for token blacklist (logout handling)
Write unit tests for token generation and validation
validation: Token validation correctly rejects expired/invalid tokens
Refresh token rotation works without race conditions
Blacklisted tokens are rejected within 1 second

steps.title: Implement OAuth2.0 Integration
steps.objective: Add OAuth2.0 login flows for Google and GitHub
actions: Implement /auth/oauth/google endpoint (authorization code flow)
Implement /auth/oauth/github endpoint (authorization code flow)
Add user profile fetching from OAuth2.0 providers
Handle account linking for existing users
Write integration tests for OAuth2.0 flows
validation: OAuth2.0 login works end-to-end in staging
User profiles correctly synced from providers
Account linking does not create duplicate users
Integration tests pass for both providers

steps.title: Implement RBAC Middleware
steps.objective: Enforce role-based access control on all API endpoints
actions: Implement RBAC middleware to check user roles/permissions
Annotate all API endpoints with required permissions
Add permission checking before resource access
Implement admin endpoints for role/permission management
Write unit and integration tests for RBAC
validation: Role changes take effect within 1 second (cache refresh)
rollback: RBAC middleware can be feature-flagged off

steps.title: Migrate Existing Users
steps.objective: Safely migrate legacy session-based users to new JWT system
prerequisites: User data export from legacy system
Migration script tested on staging data
actions: Export user data from legacy authentication system
Hash legacy passwords using new bcrypt algorithm
Bulk insert users into new user_roles table
Assign default roles based on legacy permissions
Send email notifications to users about auth changes
validation: All users migrated without data loss
Legacy passwords work with new system
rollback: Keep legacy authentication running in parallel
Feature flag to switch back to legacy system

steps.title: Performance Testing
steps.objective: Validate authentication latency and throughput under load
prerequisites: Load testing tools configured (e.g., Locust, k6)
actions: Run load tests with 10,000 concurrent users
Measure p50, p95, p99 latency for /auth/login endpoint
Test Redis cache hit rate and failover scenarios
Measure OAuth2.0 login latency
Identify and fix performance bottlenecks

steps.title: Security Audit
steps.objective: Conduct security review and penetration testing
actions: Review code for common vulnerabilities (injection, XSS, CSRF)
Test JWT token security (signature validation, expiration)
Verify OAuth2.0 implementation against RFC 6749
Test RBAC for privilege escalation vulnerabilities
Run automated security scans (Snyk, OWASP ZAP)
validation: No critical or high-severity vulnerabilities found

steps.title: Deploy to Production
steps.objective: Roll out new authentication system to production with phased deployment
actions: Deploy with feature flag (5% traffic canary)
Monitor error rates, latency, and Redis metrics
Gradually increase traffic to 25%, 50%, 100%
Enable RBAC enforcement after traffic ramp-up
Decommission legacy authentication system
validation: All users able to log in successfully
Redis cluster stable under production load
rollback: Flip feature flag to redirect to legacy system
No data loss (both systems run in parallel initially)

milestones.title: Core Authentication Features Complete
deliverables: JWT token service implemented and tested
OAuth2.0 integration with Google and GitHub
All unit and integration tests passing
exit_criteria: All authentication flows work in staging

milestones.title: Production Ready
exit_criteria: All users migrated without data loss

testing_strategy:
Multi-layered testing approach:

1. **Unit Tests**: Test individual components (token generation, validation, RBAC logic) with mocked dependencies. Target >90% coverage.

2. **Integration Tests**: Test end-to-end authentication flows including OAuth2.0 and RBAC. Use staging environment with real Redis and database.

3. **Load Tests**: Simulate 10,000 concurrent users using Locust. Measure latency (p50, p95, p99) and throughput. Test Redis failover scenarios.

4. **Security Tests**: Automated scans with OWASP ZAP and Snyk. Manual penetration testing for JWT vulnerabilities, privilege escalation, and OAuth2.0 security.

5. **User Acceptance Testing**: Beta test with 100 internal users before full rollout.
rollout_plan:
Phased rollout with feature flags:

1. **Canary (5% traffic)**: Deploy to 5% of users, monitor for 24 hours. Key metrics: error rate, latency, login success rate.

2. **Ramp-up (25%)**: If canary succeeds, increase to 25% traffic. Monitor for 24 hours.

3. **Majority (50%)**: Increase to 50% traffic. Monitor for 48 hours.

4. **Full Rollout (100%)**: Complete rollout to all users.

5. **RBAC Enforcement**: Enable RBAC after 100% traffic to new system.

6. **Legacy Decommission**: Keep legacy system running for 1 week post-rollout, then decommission.

Monitoring: Real-time dashboards for error rates, latency, Redis metrics, and login success rates. On-call engineer available during rollout.
rollback_strategy:
Multi-level rollback plan:

1. **Feature Flag Rollback**: Immediate rollback by flipping feature flag to redirect traffic to legacy authentication system. No data loss as both systems run in parallel.

2. **Code Rollback**: If feature flag fails, rollback deployment using blue-green deployment strategy. Previous version remains available.

3. **Data Rollback**: User data migration is one-way (legacy → new), so rollback involves re-enabling legacy system. No data loss as legacy database remains intact.

4. **Partial Rollback**: If issues affect specific OAuth2.0 providers, disable only that provider while keeping JWT authentication active.

Rollback triggers:
- Error rate > 1%
- p95 latency > 500ms
- Login success rate < 99%
- Critical security vulnerability discovered
